title: NOC 83100 – Underground production and development miners | GTR Immigration
description: Underground production and development miners under NOC 83100 are the specialized frontline force of Canada's world-leading mining industry. As a TEER 3 occupation, these professionals are responsible for drilling, blasting, and operating the heavy machinery required to extract precious metals, coal, and minerals from deep within the earth. In 2026, as Canada accelerates its "Critical Minerals Strategy" to power the global green energy transition, these miners are more essential than ever. For immigration, this role is a top-tier candidate for the Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP) and the new 2026 TR to PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level3Major Group83 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionSub-Major Group831 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionMinor Group8310 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionNOC Code83100

duties: Set up and operate jumbo drills or other specialized drilling machines to produce designated blasting patterns.
Load explosives, set fuses, and detonate charges to produce desired rock fragmentation in underground mines.
Operate Scooptrams, Load-Haul-Dump (LHD) machines, or mucking machines to haul ore from stopes to ore passes.
Perform safety duties such as scaling (removing loose rock from walls/roofs) and installing rock bolts or timber supports.
Extend and install air and water pipes to support the mining advance.
Inspect mine shafts and operate hoists to transport personnel and equipment.
Perform routine maintenance on heavy mining machinery and report technical issues to supervisors.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Work Experience: Previous experience as a mine labourer or in other entry-level mine support roles is usually mandatory.

Certifications:Certification in the Basic Common Core program (specifically in Ontario for hard rock mining).A provincial Blasting License may be required for those handling explosives.First Aid Certification is almost always a requirement.
Certification in the Basic Common Core program (specifically in Ontario for hard rock mining).
A provincial Blasting License may be required for those handling explosives.
First Aid Certification is almost always a requirement.
Formal training (up to six weeks) followed by extended specialized training as a "helper" is standard.
Physical stamina, mechanical aptitude, and the ability to work in confined, remote environments are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Total annual compensation for experienced miners often exceeds $120,000 - $150,000 inclusive of production bonuses and remote site premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Employment growth is driven by the opening of new critical mineral mines and the expansion of existing potash operations. The "Hiring Gap Intensity" reported by industry experts suggests that qualified underground miners will continue to have their choice of high-paying opportunities across Canada through the end of the decade.
labour_market_demand: The demand for underground miners is Very Good, specifically in northern mining hubs. In 2026, Ontario (Sudbury and Timmins), Saskatchewan (Potash and Uranium belts), and Quebec are facing significant hiring gaps. According to the Mining Industry Human Resources Council (MiHR), the sector requires thousands of new miners to meet production targets for nickel, copper, and lithium.
Global Mining Leader: Work for some of the world’s most advanced mining companies in a country known for the highest safety and environmental standards.
Technological Innovation: Gain experience with battery-electric vehicle (BEV) fleets, remote-controlled scooptrams, and automated drilling systems.
High Earning Potential: Underground mining is one of the highest-paying skilled trades in Canada, often featuring "Fly-In-Fly-Out" (FIFO) schedules with paid travel and lodging.
Stable PR Pathways: This NOC is frequently included in Express Entry Category-Based Draws for "Trade Occupations."
pathways: Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): A direct pathway for experienced miners who meet the language and work experience requirements.
Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Targeted for candidates in the "Trade Occupations" category.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure for workers already in Canada contributing to essential sectors like natural resources.
Saskatchewan INP (Occupations In-Demand): High priority for the province's massive potash and uranium sectors.
